incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

steps.text: Count the boxes. Then count what's in each box.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: Groups and items slots highlight in the equation template.

steps.text: Look at the boxes. How many boxes? That's the first number. Count the crayons in one box. That's the second number. Then skip count by 7s: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. That's the product.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the boxes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six boxes. That's my first number. Now I count one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. Seven crayons. That's my second number. 6 times 7. And the product? Skip count by 7s six times: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. The equation is 6 times 7 equals 42. That's how equal groups connect to multiplication.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

steps.text: Look closely. How many bags? How many stars in each?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

steps.text: Count the bags: 5. Count the stars in one bag: 9. So 5 times 9. Skip count by 9s: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. Place those three numbers.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight one at a time as guide counts them.

steps.text: Here's how it works. I count the bags: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five bags total. Then I pick one bag and count the stars inside: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Nine stars. So I have 5 groups of 9. 5 times 9. To find the product, I skip count by 9s: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. The answer is 5 times 9 equals 45. Five bags, 9 in each, same value as 45.
